data.name: Classic Caprese Salad
data.description: A vibrant, refreshing Italian salad featuring ripe tomatoes, creamy fresh mozzarella, and sweet basil leaves, finished with a drizzle of quality olive oil and a pinch of flaky sea salt.

refrigerator.notes: Tomatoes degrade in the refrigerator; this salad is best eaten immediately.

items.name: ripe tomatoes
items.notes: medium to large size, preferably vine-ripened or heirloom, approximately 450g total

items.name: fresh mozzarella cheese
items.notes: buffalo mozzarella or fior di latte

instructions.text: Using a sharp chef's knife or serrated knife, slice the tomatoes and fresh mozzarella into even rounds, approximately one centimeter thick.
Arrange the tomato and mozzarella slices on a serving platter, alternating them in a slightly overlapping circular or linear pattern.
Tuck the fresh basil leaves evenly between the overlapping slices of tomato and mozzarella.
Sprinkle the arranged salad evenly with the flaky sea salt and freshly ground black pepper.
Drizzle the extra virgin olive oil generously over the entire platter just before serving.

troubleshooting.likely_cause: Tomatoes were too wet or mozzarella leaked excess whey after slicing
troubleshooting.prevention: Pat mozzarella slices gently with a paper towel before assembling, and use firm but ripe tomatoes
troubleshooting.fix: Carefully dab the pooling liquid on the platter with a paper towel before serving

troubleshooting.likely_cause: Basil was chopped with a dull knife or handled too roughly
troubleshooting.prevention: Leave basil leaves whole, or tear them gently by hand just before serving
troubleshooting.fix: Replace damaged basil leaves with fresh ones

chef_notes: Always serve Caprese salad at room temperature. Refrigeration mutes the natural sweetness of the tomatoes and makes the fresh mozzarella rubbery.

Do not use balsamic vinegar in a traditional Caprese salad. The acidity clashes with the delicate sweetness of the tomatoes and the pure flavor of the olive oil. Let the high-quality ingredients speak for themselves.

The quality of the extra virgin olive oil is critical here. It acts as a primary flavor component rather than a cooking medium, so choose an oil with a grassy, peppery, or fruity profile that you enjoy.
data.cultural_context: The Insalata Caprese originates from the island of Capri in the Campania region of Italy, where it became popular in the mid-twentieth century. Its simple ingredients intentionally represent the red, white, and green colors of the Italian flag, making it a patriotic dish. It perfectly encapsulates the Italian culinary philosophy of relying on a few pristine, high-quality, seasonal ingredients rather than complex techniques.
